title: Your First Real Yes
developmental_frame: At 11–12, money is becoming real but is still often secondary to the pride of *making something people want*. A child this age can make one good thing and offer it, but handling many customers, real pricing strategy, or rejection from strangers is a stretch — the first 'yes' often comes from someone they know, and that's developmentally right. Keep stakes tiny and real, and keep the loop short so the yes arrives quickly.
authentic_value_meaning: Value at this age means someone *chooses* to have the thing you made — a real, voluntary yes, even from a neighbour or aunt, as long as it isn't just to be kind. The lesson isn't profit; it's the discovery that making something good and offering it can lead to a genuine 'yes, I'd like that'.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child makes one real thing (a baked treat, a small craft, a simple helper service) and aims for one honest yes from a real person. The stakes are small but real — a real person really deciding — which feels different from pretend play. Landing the first genuine yes is a memorable, motivating milestone at this age.

artifact: One real thing made or one real service offered, plus a record of the first genuine yes (who, what they said) and any nos with their reasons.
success_criteria: The child made or prepared something real to offer.
They made a real ask to at least one person outside the immediate household.
They can say why the person said yes or no, in the person's own words.

lessons.title: What do people around you actually want?
lessons.concept: You can only sell or give something people actually want. So start by *noticing* — what do the people near you wish they had, or wish was easier?
lessons.real_example: A kid noticed her neighbours' plants dying while they were at work. 'I'll water your plants for RM5 a week' was an offer people actually wanted — because she spotted a real wish first.
lessons.apply_task: Watch the people around you for a day. Write down three things they seem to want or find annoying. Circle one you could help with.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is the thing you circled something *they* want, or something *you'd* enjoy making? The best ideas are both — but their want has to be real.
lessons.feedback_task: Ask the person: 'Would something like this be useful to you?' Write down their actual words.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a sibling why you should find out what people want *before* making something.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you think of ideas after you've watched real people — but it can't see your neighbours. The noticing has to be yours; that's the actual skill.
success_criteria: Three real wants are noted and one is chosen based on a real person's want.

lessons.title: Make one good one
lessons.concept: Beginners rush to make ten of something and they're all a bit rubbish. Make *one*, and make it genuinely good. One good thing earns a yes; ten rushed ones earn nos.
lessons.real_example: A boy selling friendship bracelets made one beautiful sample first. People saw the good one and *then* ordered — the quality did the selling.
lessons.apply_task: Make one really good version of your thing (or plan your service carefully). Take your time; this one has to be good enough to earn a yes.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Would *you* say yes to your own thing? If not, what needs to be better before you offer it?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your one good version to someone honest and ask what would make it better. Improve it once.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell a friend why 'one good one' beats 'ten rushed ones'.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: If your thing is digital, AI might help you make it — but you must be able to make it good *and* understand it. For a hands-made thing, the craft is yours; don't fake quality you didn't build.
success_criteria: One genuinely good version exists, improved at least once after feedback.

lessons.title: What's it worth?
lessons.concept: A price (or a trade) is you saying what your thing is worth. Too cheap and it feels worthless; too dear and people say no. You're looking for a fair number that feels right to both sides.
lessons.real_example: The plant-watering kid started at RM2 and everyone said yes instantly — too cheap. At RM5, people still said yes, and she earned more for the same work. The price was a question she tested.
lessons.apply_task: Decide a fair price or trade for your thing and write one sentence on why. Think about the time and materials it took.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Does your price feel a little scary to say out loud? A price that feels slightly bold is often about right.
lessons.feedback_task: Say your price to one person and watch their reaction before they answer. Note whether they blinked.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why a price that everyone accepts instantly might be too low.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can tell you what similar things cost, as a rough guide. But the fair price depends on your effort and your real people — trust what they show you over what the model guesses.
success_criteria: A price or trade is set with a stated reason.

lessons.title: The first ask
lessons.concept: Everything so far was getting ready. It becomes real when you actually ask someone: 'Would you like this?' A yes is exciting; a no isn't failure — it usually comes with a reason you can learn from.
lessons.real_example: A girl selling cookies heard 'no thanks, I don't like chocolate' — so her next batch had a plain option, and that person bought. The no taught her the fix.
lessons.apply_task: Make your real ask to at least one person outside your home. Write down what they said — yes or no — and their reason.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did asking feel? If a no stung, remember: it's information, not a verdict on you. What did it teach you?
lessons.feedback_task: Share your yes/no and the reason with a parent or mentor, and decide together one thing to try differently next time.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the story of your first ask — the yes or the no — and what you learned from it.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: No tool can make the ask for you, and that's the point — the small nervousness of a real ask is exactly the thing you're practising. Use AI only afterward, to help think through what a no was telling you.
success_criteria: A real ask was made to someone outside the household.
The response and its reason were recorded, and one next step was chosen.
